subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2! In this unit you will become confident with one of the most useful skills in all of mathematics: telling, measuring and planning with time. You already use time every single day. When Amina wakes before dawn for the Fajr prayer, when Yusuf waits for the bus to Eastleigh, or when Halima checks how long the bariis needs to cook, they are all thinking about time. By the end of this unit you will read clocks, switch between the 12-hour and 24-hour systems, work out how long things last, and use real timetables to plan journeys between cities like Mogadishu, Hargeisa and Nairobi.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 4 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name the main units of time and convert between them (seconds, minutes, hours, days, weeks, months, years).
Read the time on an analogue clock and a digital clock.
Understand AM and PM and know what happens at noon and midnight.
Work out a time interval or duration – how long there is between two times, even across the hour or across noon.
Use a calendar: know the number of days in each month, weeks, and leap years.
Read and interpret timetables in both 12-hour and 24-hour formats.
Plan a journey using a timetable and calculate how long it lasts.
Solve real-world problems involving time and timetables.

concepts.title: Units of Time and Converting Between Them
concepts.explanation: Time is measured in units, and each unit is built from the one below it. Here are the units you need, from smallest to largest: Second – the smallest unit we use every day. A single heartbeat is about one second.
concepts.example: Month – about 4 weeks, or 28 to 31 days, make 1 month.

concepts.title: Reading an Analogue Clock
concepts.explanation: An analogue clock has a round face with the numbers 1 to 12 spaced evenly around it, and two moving hands. Learning to read it well is like learning to read a special map. The short hand is the hour hand. It tells you which hour it is.
concepts.example: The long hand is the minute hand. It tells you how many minutes past the hour it is.

concepts.title: Reading a Digital Clock
concepts.explanation: A digital clock shows the time as numbers, usually with a colon (:) between the hours and the minutes. The number before the colon is the hour; the number after it is the minutes. It is often quicker to read than an analogue clock because you do not have to work out where a hand is pointing. For example, a digital clock showing 7:15 means 7 hours and 15 minutes, which is the same as quarter past 7. A clock showing 8:30 means 8 hours and 30 minutes, or half past 8. One thing to be careful about: the minutes always…
concepts.example: Being able to move between analogue and digital is a real sign of understanding. If Farah's wall clock (analogue) shows the hour hand between 9 and 10 with the minute hand on 9, she can write it on her phone (digital)…

concepts.title: AM and PM
concepts.explanation: A day has 24 hours, but an ordinary clock face only shows 12. That means the clock passes through each number twice in one day – once in the morning and once in the afternoon or evening. To tell the two apart in the 12-hour system, we use the labels AM and PM. AM stands for the Latin *ante meridiem*, meaning 'before midday'. AM covers the times from midnight (12:00 AM) up to just before noon (11:59 AM) – the morning hours.
concepts.example: PM stands for *post meridiem*, meaning 'after midday'. PM covers the times from noon (12:00 PM) up to just before midnight (11:59 PM) – the afternoon and evening hours.

concepts.title: The 24-Hour Clock
concepts.explanation: There is another way to show time that never needs AM or PM: the 24-hour clock. Instead of counting to 12 twice, it counts the hours straight through from 0 to 23. Midnight is 00:00, and the hours keep climbing all day: 1 in the morning is 01:00, noon is 12:00, 1 in the afternoon is 13:00, and so on up to 23:00, which is 11 o'clock at night. The 24-hour clock is used on train and bus timetables, on many phones, and by the army, because it can never be misread. One helpful rule: in the 24-hour clock you always…

concepts.title: Working Out Durations and Time Intervals
concepts.explanation: A duration (also called an interval) is how much time passes between two moments. This is one of the most useful time skills, because real life is full of questions like 'How long is the journey?' or 'How long until iftar?' There is a safe method that always works. Make sure both times are in the same system (both 12-hour or both 24-hour). If they cross from AM to PM, using the 24-hour clock often makes it easier.
concepts.example: Count on from the earlier time to the next whole hour.

methods.title: How to work out a duration
methods.example: How many minutes are there in 3.5 hours?
steps: Put both times in the same system (both 12-hour or both 24-hour).
Count on from the earlier time to the next whole hour.
Count on in whole hours.
Count on the extra minutes to reach the later time.
Add the parts together.

methods.title: How to convert a unit of time
methods.example: The hour hand is between 8 and 9, and the minute hand points to 3. What is the time?
steps: Decide if you are going from a larger unit to a smaller one, or the other way.
Larger to smaller: multiply by 60, 24, 7 or 12 as needed.
Smaller to larger: divide by 60, 24, 7 or 12 as needed.
If it does not divide evenly, give the answer as a whole unit plus a remainder.

methods.title: How to read a timetable
steps: Find the row for your stop or station.
Find the column for the service you want.
Read across and down to find departure and arrival times.
Work out the duration by counting on from departure to arrival.

methods.title: Convert 24-hour to 12-hour
steps: Solution: The hour 14 is greater than 12, so it is a PM time and we subtract 12: 14 − 12 = 2.
The answer is 2:30 PM.
Check the convert 24-hour to 12-hour result carefully.

methods.title: Work out a duration
methods.example: How long is it from 11:20 AM to 2:05 PM?
steps: Solution: Count on.
From 11:20 AM to 1:20 PM is 2 hours.
From 1:20 PM to 2:05 PM is 45 minutes.
Total: 2 hours and 45 minutes.

methods.title: Find an end time
methods.example: A film starts at 6:30 PM and lasts 2 hours 15 minutes. What time does it end?
steps: Solution: Add the hours first: 6:30 PM + 2 hours = 8:30 PM.
Then add the minutes: 8:30 PM + 15 minutes = 8:45 PM.
Check the find an end time result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Convert a unit of time
workedExamples.prompt: How many minutes are there in 3.5 hours?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: 3.5 hours means 3 hours and a half hour. 3 whole hours = 3 × 60 = 180 minutes. Half an hour = 30 minutes. Add them: 180 + 30 = 210 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Read an analogue clock
workedExamples.prompt: The hour hand is between 8 and 9, and the minute hand points to 3. What is the time?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The hour hand has passed 8 but not reached 9, so the hour is 8. The minute hand on 3 means 3 × 5 = 15 minutes. So the time is 8:15, which we say as quarter past 8.

workedExamples.title: Convert 12-hour to 24-hour
workedExamples.solution: Solution: It is a PM time, so add 12 to the hours: 7 + 12 = 19. The minutes stay the same. The answer is 19:20.

workedExamples.title: Convert 24-hour to 12-hour
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The hour 14 is greater than 12, so it is a PM time and we subtract 12: 14 − 12 = 2. The answer is 2:30 PM.

workedExamples.title: Work out a duration
workedExamples.prompt: How long is it from 11:20 AM to 2:05 PM?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count on. From 11:20 AM to 1:20 PM is 2 hours. From 1:20 PM to 2:05 PM is 45 minutes. Total: 2 hours and 45 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Find an end time
workedExamples.prompt: A film starts at 6:30 PM and lasts 2 hours 15 minutes. What time does it end?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Add the hours first: 6:30 PM + 2 hours = 8:30 PM. Then add the minutes: 8:30 PM + 15 minutes = 8:45 PM.

workedExamples.title: Read a timetable
workedExamples.prompt: Using the bus timetable above, how long does the evening bus take from the Town Centre (18:30) to the Hospital (19:00)?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count on from 18:30 to 19:00. That is 30 minutes. The journey takes 30 minutes.

workedExamples.title: Plan a journey
workedExamples.prompt: Yusuf must reach the masjid by 13:00 for the Dhuhr prayer. The walk takes 20 minutes. What is the latest time he should set off?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Count back 20 minutes from 13:00. 13:00 minus 20 minutes is 12:40. So Yusuf should leave no later than 12:40, and a little earlier would be wiser.

workedExamples.title: Write the time shown
workedExamples.prompt: Write the time shown: a) hour hand on 3, minute hand on 12 b) hour hand between 7 and 8, minute hand on 6.
workedExamples.solution: a) three o'clock (3:00), b) half past seven (7:30).

workedExamples.title: Practice 10
workedExamples.prompt: How many days are in September, in February (normal year), and in July?

workedExamples.title: Practice 11
workedExamples.prompt: A train leaves at 09:45 and arrives at 11:15. How long is the journey?

workedExamples.title: Practice 12
workedExamples.prompt: Use this train timetable to answer questions 1 to 3.
workedExamples.solution: 1) Train 203 (it reaches East at 11:20, before 11:30; the next train, 305, arrives 14:05, too late).

activities.title: Make a Paper Clock
activities.materials: a paper plate or a circle of card, a pencil, a split pin or a paper fastener, and two paper hands (one short, one long).
steps: Write the numbers 1 to 12 evenly around the edge of the circle.
Cut out a short hour hand and a long minute hand and pin them to the centre so they can turn.
Set your clock to different times: quarter past 3, half past 7, quarter to 10.
Ask a family member to call out a time and set it on your clock as fast as you can.
Keep your paper clock – you can use it all through this unit to practise reading and showing times.

activities.title: The 24-Hour Conversion Game
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and a partner.
steps: Each player writes ten times on small cards – some in 12-hour form (with AM or PM) and some in 24-hour form.
Shuffle the cards and place them face down in a pile.
Take turns to draw a card and convert the time to the other system out loud.
If you are right, keep the card. The player with more cards at the end wins.
This game makes the add-12 and subtract-12 rules become quick and automatic.

activities.title: Design Your Ideal Daily Timetable
activities.materials: paper, a ruler and a pencil.
steps: Draw a table with two columns: 'Time' and 'Activity'.
Plan your ideal day from waking up to bedtime, including the five prayer times, meals, school, football and rest.
Write every time in 24-hour format so there is no confusion.
Work out and write down how long each activity lasts.
Share your timetable with your teacher at your next session and explain how you worked out the durations.

activities.title: Timetable Investigation
activities.materials: a real bus, train or prayer-time timetable (ask an adult, or use one from a phone).
steps: Choose a starting place and a destination on the timetable.
Write down the departure and arrival times.
Work out how long the journey lasts by counting on.
Now plan a return trip: if you must be back home by a certain time, which service should you take?

activities.title: How Long Does It Take? (Family Challenge)
activities.materials: a clock or a phone with a timer, and your family.
steps: Choose everyday tasks: making anjero, walking to the shop, tidying the room.
Guess (estimate) how many minutes each task will take.
Time each task and write down the real duration.
Compare your estimate to the real time. Whose guesses were closest?

activities.title: Calendar Detective
steps: Find how many days are in each month and check them against the rhyme.
Count how many days there are until the next Eid or a family birthday.
Find out which day of the week Ramadan begins on this year.
Work out how many weeks there are between two dates you choose.

After your activities, talk or write about these questions: Which activity helped you understand the 24-hour clock best? What was the trickiest part of working out durations? What would you like more practice with? Share your answers with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: Key rule 1
rules.text: Units of Time 60 seconds = 1 minute. 60 minutes = 1 hour. 24 hours = 1 day. 7 days = 1 week. 12 months = 1 year (365 days, or 366 in a leap year). Larger to smaller: multiply. Smaller to larger: divide.

rules.title: AM and PM Rule
rules.text: 12:00 AM is midnight. 12:00 PM is noon. AM = morning (before midday). PM = afternoon and evening (after midday).

rules.title: 24-Hour Clock Rule
rules.text: 12-hour PM to 24-hour: add 12 to the hours (except 12 PM stays 12:00). 24-hour to 12-hour after noon: subtract 12 from the hours. Midnight is 00:00. Always write four digits, such as 07:05.

terms: A round-faced clock with moving hands
A clock that shows the time in numbers
Hours counted 0 to 23, no AM or PM
How long there is between two times
A chart of times services run or events happen
When something leaves / when it arrives
A year with 366 days (29 days in February)
commonMistakes: 12:00 AM is midnight, 12:00 PM is noon
PM to 24-hour: add 12; after noon back to 12-hour: subtract 12
Use the rhyme: 30 for Sept, Apr, Jun, Nov

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Use this train timetable to answer questions 1 to 3.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) Train 203 (it reaches East at 11:20, before 11:30; the next train, 305, arrives 14:05, too late).
Central to West is 08:00 to 09:40 = 1 hour 40 minutes (the same on every train).
Converting all to 24-hour: 14:50, 14:55 (2:55 PM), 15:00 (3:00 PM), 15:05 – so the order earliest to latest is 14:50, 2:55 PM, 3:00 PM, 15:05.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: You need to be at East station by 11:30 AM. Which train should you take?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Use the section 3: challenge guidance and explain each step.

selfAssessment: I can name the main units of time and convert between them (seconds, minutes, hours, days, weeks, months, years).
I can read the time on an analogue clock and a digital clock.
I can understand AM and PM and know what happens at noon and midnight.
I can convert between 12-hour and 24-hour time confidently.
I can work out a time interval or duration – how long there is between two times, even across the hour or across noon.
I can use a calendar: know the number of days in each month, weeks, and leap years.
I can read and interpret timetables in both 12-hour and 24-hour formats.
I can plan a journey using a timetable and calculate how long it lasts.
